children.name: Tachybaptus
children.description: Little grebes — the smallest and most compact of all grebes, found on a much wider range of water bodies than their larger relatives. The little grebe (smådopping) is a remarkably buoyant bird, riding high on the water like a cork and diving suddenly with a smooth, almost silent plunge. Despite its tiny size it is feisty and territorial, driving away intruders many times its own size with a loud, shrill whinny. Little grebes are one of the most widespread wetland birds in Sweden, found on ponds as small as a few hundred square metres — anywhere there is aquatic vegetation for nesting and small fish or invertebrates to eat. In autumn they can aggregate in small flocks on larger lakes before moving to coastal wintering areas.

children.name: Tachybaptus ruficollis
children.description: The smådopping — Europe's smallest grebe, barely larger than a starling. Its rotund, tailless silhouette and habit of bouncing back to the surface after each dive make it instantly recognisable. Surprisingly widespread and common in Sweden, nesting on ponds, ditches, slow rivers, and urban park lakes. Its loud, descending whinny — a cascading trill audible across the whole wetland — is one of the characteristic sounds of a Swedish spring marsh.

children.name: Tachybaptus novaehollandiae
children.description: A small grebe found throughout Australia, New Zealand, and islands of the southwestern Pacific, common on freshwater ponds and reservoirs. In breeding plumage it has a golden-brown cheek fan and bright yellow eye.

children.name: Tachybaptus pelzelnii
children.description: A small grebe endemic to Madagascar, found on shallow freshwater lakes and marshes in the interior. Its population has declined sharply due to introduced fish and habitat degradation; IUCN status: Vulnerable.

children.name: Tachybaptus dominicus
children.description: The smallest grebe in the Americas, a tiny greyish bird of tropical and subtropical freshwater wetlands from Texas to Argentina. It is secretive and easily overlooked but widespread in suitable habitat.

children.name: Tachybaptus rufolavatus
children.description: A small grebe endemic to Lake Alaotra in eastern Madagascar, distinguished by its pale brown plumage and dark cap. Flightless or nearly so, it was driven to extinction by introduced carnivorous fish, habitat degradation, and entanglement in fishing nets. No confirmed sighting since 1985; declared Extinct by the IUCN in 2010.

children.name: Podilymbus
children.description: One or two species of stocky grebes with a distinctive thick, chicken-like bill, breeding in the Americas. The Pied-billed Grebe is one of the most widely distributed waterbirds in the New World, and its habit of slowly sinking into the water like a submarine when disturbed gives it the colloquial name 'hell-diver'.

children.name: Podilymbus podiceps
children.description: A compact, brownish grebe with a distinctive thick bill encircled by a black band in breeding plumage. Widespread across freshwater habitats from Canada to Argentina. Secretive but common, it is often heard before seen — a loud, whooping call that carries across the marsh.

children.name: Podilymbus gigas
children.description: A large, flightless grebe endemic to Lake Atitlán in the Guatemalan highlands. Its population collapsed after the introduction of smallmouth bass and catastrophic lake-level changes following the 1976 earthquake. Last reported in 1986; declared Extinct by the IUCN in 1994.

children.name: Rollandia
children.description: South American grebes of the high Andes and lowland wetlands, named after French naturalist Rolland. The Titicaca Grebe is one of the largest grebes and is flightless, confined to Lake Titicaca and surrounding highland lakes.

children.name: Rollandia rolland
children.description: A small grebe of southern South America with a distinctive white ear-tuft extending from the eye rearwards. Found from Peru and Brazil to Tierra del Fuego, inhabiting freshwater lakes and marshes across a wide elevational range.

children.name: Rollandia microptera
children.description: A large, flightless grebe endemic to Lake Titicaca and a few neighbouring lakes in the high Andes of Peru and Bolivia. It relies entirely on its strong legs for movement and is vulnerable to drowning in fishing nets; IUCN status: Endangered.

children.name: Poliocephalus
children.description: Two species of Australasian grebes with fine silvery-white streaking on the head, named from the Greek for 'grey head'. They breed in freshwater lakes and are notable for elaborate weed-presentation courtship displays similar to those of Podiceps.

children.name: Poliocephalus poliocephalus
children.description: An Australian grebe whose grey head and neck are streaked white, giving a hoary appearance. It forms large flocks on open lakes and estuaries in winter and performs a high-speed 'pattering' courtship display across the water surface.

children.name: Poliocephalus rufopectus
children.description: A small grebe endemic to New Zealand, confined largely to the North Island. Its rich chestnut breast and cheek patch make it one of the most colourful grebes. Populations have declined with wetland drainage; IUCN status: Near Threatened.

children.name: Aechmophorus
children.description: Two large, elegant grebes of North America with long slender necks and bills, formerly considered a single species. They breed colonially on freshwater lakes and winter on the Pacific coast. Their spectacular 'rushing' courtship ceremony, in which the pair runs side-by-side across the water upright and in perfect synchrony, is one of the most famous displays in the bird world.

children.name: Aechmophorus occidentalis
children.description: A large black-and-white grebe of western North American lakes and Pacific coastal waters, with a long, swan-like neck and bright red eye. Famous for the 'rushing' courtship display in which pairs sprint across the water in perfect synchrony, bodies erect and bills pointed forward.

children.name: Aechmophorus clarkii
children.description: Very similar to the Western Grebe but with a brighter white face and orange-yellow bill. It was only recognised as a distinct species in the 1980s after studies revealed consistent differences in plumage, bill colour, and courtship calls.

children.name: Podiceps
children.description: The large grebes — superbly adapted diving birds with lobed feet set so far back on the body that they are virtually unable to walk on land. Grebes are among the most accomplished divers of all freshwater birds, using their feet rather than wings to propel themselves underwater in pursuit of fish. The great crested grebe's elaborate courtship display — the most complex of any Swedish waterbird — involves both partners rising chest-to-chest from the water in the 'penguin dance', shaking their spectacular rufous-and-black crests and exchanging gifts of waterweed. This ceremony was first scientifically described by Julian Huxley in 1914 and remains a textbook example of ritualized courtship behaviour. The grebes' beautiful plumage made them targets for the 19th-century feather trade; their recovery catalysed early British bird protection legislation.

children.name: Podiceps cristatus
children.description: The skäggdopping — Sweden's largest grebe and one of its most beautiful birds. The elaborate head-shaking courtship dance in early spring, performed by both sexes with erect rufous-and-black crests, is one of the most spectacular sights on Swedish lakes. Nests on sheltered bays of larger lakes; chicks ride on the parents' backs beneath the wing feathers. Once hunted nearly to extinction for its silky breast plumage, which was used to trim Victorian hats; now common and increasing across lowland Sweden.

children.name: Podiceps grisegena
children.description: The gråhakedopping — a declining breeding species in southern and central Sweden, requiring larger eutrophic lakes with rich fish populations. In breeding plumage its chestnut-red neck, grey cheeks, and yellow bill base make it unmistakeable. Less showy in courtship than its great crested relative but equally devoted to its mate, with pairs duetting in loud braying calls across breeding lakes. Winters on sheltered Swedish coastal bays and the southern Baltic.

children.name: Podiceps auritus
children.description: The svarthakedopping — arguably the most stunning of all Swedish waterbirds in breeding plumage, with brilliant golden-orange ear-tufts that fan out from behind the eye like flames against a black cap. A scarce breeder in northern Sweden, particularly in Lapland where it nests on small forest pools and lochans. Winters on the open sea off the Swedish coast, where it is drably grey and white. Population trends are of conservation concern across its Scandinavian range.

children.name: Podiceps nigricollis
children.description: The svarthalsad dopping — a small, elegant grebe whose breeding plumage features a black head and neck with a spectacular fan of golden-yellow ear-coverts. An irregular breeder in Sweden, it favours shallow eutrophic lakes with abundant emergent vegetation. Winters on open coasts and the Mediterranean.

children.name: Podiceps occipitalis
children.description: A small grebe of the Andes and Patagonian steppes, with a pale silvery-grey head and neck and a bright yellow bill. It is common on high-altitude lakes and lagoons from Ecuador to Tierra del Fuego.

children.name: Podiceps taczanowskii
children.description: A medium-sized grebe found only in Lake Junín in the high Andes of Peru. Flightless or nearly so, it is highly vulnerable to water-level fluctuations and pollution from mining operations; IUCN status: Endangered.

children.name: Podiceps gallardoi
children.description: One of the rarest grebes in the world, breeding only on a few remote lakes in the windswept steppes of Patagonian Argentina. Its striking black-and-white hood and maroon chest make it unmistakable. The population has declined catastrophically due to introduced trout and American mink; IUCN status: Critically Endangered.

children.name: Podiceps major
children.description: The largest Neotropical grebe, found from Peru to southern Chile and Argentina. It has a dark grey body, long chestnut neck, and black crown. Unlike most grebes it often inhabits coastal estuaries and sheltered bays.

children.name: Podiceps andinus
children.description: A small grebe endemic to the high-altitude wetlands of the Colombian Andes, restricted to Lake Tota and surrounding marshes. It was driven to extinction by wetland drainage, eutrophication, competition from introduced rainbow trout, and hunting. Last confirmed sighting in 1977; declared Extinct by the IUCN in 1994.
